content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Trace Prompting for Robust Code Generation in LLMs",
  "problem": "Standard code generation prompting relies heavily on optimal or canonical code examples, which may lead to overfitting and poor generalization to buggy or adversarial inputs. There is limited exploration of how LLMs handle erroneous or counterfactual code traces within prompts.",
  "motivation": "Improving code generation robustness is critical as LLMs are increasingly used in real-world software development, where encountering non-canonical and buggy code is common. By challenging models with atypical or erroneous code traces, we can potentially force them to internalize deeper program semantics. This is timely due to the growing prevalence of code LLMs and the need for safer, more reliable code synthesis.",
  "method": "We propose Counterfactual Trace Prompting (CTP), a prompting method that injects minimally perturbed, buggy, or logically altered code snippets as few-shot exemplars alongside canonical ones. The prompt explicitly asks the model to 'generate correct code given the following buggy and correct examples.' The perturbations are systematically generated based on static analysis (e.g., off-by-one errors, swapped variables), and are labeled as such in the prompt. This approach hypothesizes that exposure to erroneous traces will force LLMs to learn discriminative features of correct code, improving their resilience and accuracy.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate CTP on standard code generation datasets (e.g., HumanEval, MBPP) and newly curated sets with adversarial bugs. Baselines include standard few-shot prompting and chain-of-thought prompting. Metrics will cover functional correctness, robustness to adversarial inputs, and error type recognition. Ablation studies will vary the type and frequency of counterfactual traces."
}
